source.title: A plausible role of imagination in pretend play, counterfactual reasoning, and executive functions

confidence_rationale: Structural equation modelling with N=189 children (mean age 4.8 years) shows pretend play and counterfactual reasoning load onto separate but significantly correlated latent constructs (r=.51, p=.001), both predicted by a higher-order latent imaginative capacity. EF accounts for unique variance in both (beta=.21 and .22 respectively). This is the most methodologically rigorous empirical test of the Weisberg-Gopnik imagination-as-shared-substrate hypothesis. Direct relevance to MECH-198: the imagination latent factor is what REE would call the hypothesis-tag system exercised across both task types. Confidence slightly reduced because the 'imagination' latent is inferred from structural model fit rather than independently measured; the model shows good but not excellent fit (CFI=.94, RMSEA=.08).
failure_signatures: If EF were the only driver (no shared imagination construct), the SEM model would fit equally well without the imagination latent factor -- but model fit degrades substantially when imagination is removed, supporting a distinct imaginative capacity beyond EF.

Object substitution, referring to absent objects, verbal property attribution, and sequenced imaginary actions are the behavioral indicators of the pretend play construct -- agents lacking any of these show reduced counterfactual transfer, each mapping to distinct aspects of REE's frame+hypothesis_tag architecture.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In children aged ~5 years, pretend play (PP) and counterfactual reasoning (CFR) load onto distinct but correlated latent constructs (r=.51); a higher-order imaginative capacity predicts both; complex executive function (inhibition + working memory + cognitive flexibility combined) accounts for unique variance in both after language is controlled.
mapping.ree_translation: The shared 'imaginative capacity' latent is REE's hypothesis tag system (MECH-094) -- the agent-level capacity to generate and maintain counterfactual/synthetic representations decoupled from the real-consequence stream. EF requirements in both tasks reflect the cognitive cost of maintaining dual representation (one real, one synthetic) without frame confusion -- exactly the commitment architecture exercise that MECH-198 predicts pretend play trains.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study measures imagination as an inferred latent from behavioral indicators, not via direct neurobiological measurement. The specific EF components contributing are complex EF (multi-factorial), not decomposed into inhibition alone or working memory alone -- this limits mapping to specific REE substrate mechanisms.
mapping.source_context: N=189, ages 4-5, UK. Confirmatory factor analysis + SEM. Good model fit. Ecologically valid pretend play observation + standardized CFR tasks.

confidence_components.notes: Good source quality: British Journal of Psychology, SEM methodology, adequate N. Good mapping fidelity: imagination latent directly maps to hypothesis_tag conceptually. Low transfer risk: same developmental population.
